pi_items.source: Code analysis: consistent across setup_task.sh (OUTPUT_PATH), export_result.sh (OUTPUT_PATH), and verifier.py (expected_output variable)
Web search and ImageJ documentation confirm 'Fly Brain' is a built-in sample image accessible via File > Open Samples > Fly Brain in Fiji/ImageJ. Reference: https://imagej.net/plugins/samples
Web search results confirm the Fly Brain sample image is 256x256 pixels. The sample is a confocal stack with 57 slices of 256x256 pixel images
Web search results confirm the Fly Brain sample image is 256x256 pixels (same dimensions as width)

Code analysis: setup_task.sh defines GT_MIP_PATH variable as '/var/lib/imagej/ground_truth/fly_brain_mip.tif' and generates this file by running ImageJ macro to create Max Intensity Projection of Fly Brain sample

privileged_info_summary: The task uses the 'Fly Brain' sample, a real confocal microscopy stack included with Fiji/ImageJ showing a Drosophila larval brain. The sample consists of 57 slices, each 256x256 pixels. The task instructs users to apply the Temporal-Color Code tool (Image > Hyperstacks > Temporal-Color Code) with a spectrum LUT to generate a depth-coded color projection where color represents Z-depth. The output should be saved as PNG to /home/ga/ImageJ_Data/results/depth_coded_fly_brain.png. The setup script generates a Max Intensity Projection ground truth reference at /var/lib/imagej/ground_truth/fly_brain_mip.tif for structural comparison during verification. The verifier checks that the output matches the ground truth structure and verifies actual depth coding was applied (rather than just applying a LUT to a simple projection) by analyzing the correlation between hue and image intensity.
